|# Letters to Imlay

[](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&printsec=frontcover&source=gbs_ge_summary_r&cad=0)

[Mary Wollstonecraft](https://www.google.com/search?tbo=p&tbm=bks&q=inauthor:%22Mary+Wollstonecraft%22)

C. Kegan Paul & Company , 1879 - [Women authors, English](https://www.google.com/search?tbo=p&tbm=bks&q=subject:%22Women+authors,+English%22&source=gbs_ge_summary_r&cad=0) \- 207 pages

These private correspondences of one of history's most eminent feminist thinkers reveal the intellectual and social climate of late 18th century England, and provide a personal look at Wollstonecraft.

### Popular passages

[Page 27](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA27&vq=%22to+belong+to+her.+When+men+get+immersed+in+the+world,+they+seem+to+lose+all+sensations,+excepting+those+necessary%22&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4) \- Considering the care and anxiety a woman must have about her child before it comes into the world, it seems to me, by a natural right, to belong to her. When men get immersed in the world, they seem to lose all sensations, excepting those necessary to continue or produce life! Are these the privileges of reason? Amongst the feathered race, whilst the hen keeps the young warm, her mate stays by to cheer her; but it is sufficient for man to condescend to get a child, in order to claim it. (Wollstonecraft... ‎

[Appears in 25 books from 1879-2005](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&qtid=c7e78fd&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4)

[Page liv](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&pg=PR54&vq=%22The+partiality+we+conceived+for+each+other+was+in+that+mode+which+I+have+always+considered+as+the+purest+and%22&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4) \- The partiality we conceived for each other was in that mode which I have always considered as the purest and most refined style of love. It grew with equal advances in the mind of each. It would have been impossible for the most minute observer to have said who was before and who was after. One sex did not take the priority which long-established custom has awarded it, nor the other overstep that delicacy which is so severely imposed. ‎

[Appears in 44 books from 1798-2008](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&qtid=d07e78e2&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4)

More

[Page xxvi](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&pg=PR26&vq=%22built+on+this+simple+principle,+that+if+woman+be+not+prepared+by+education+to+become+the+companion+of+man,+she%22&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4) \- Contending for the rights of woman, my main argument is built on this simple principle, that if she be not prepared by education to become the companion of man, she will stop the progress of knowledge and virtue; for truth must be common to all, or it will be inefficacious with respect to its influence on general practice. ‎

[Appears in 76 books from 1837-2008](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&qtid=e57e78d5&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4)

[Page 10](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA10&vq=%22word+for+desirable+%3B+and,+beyond+these+casual+ebullitions+of+sympathy,+few+look+for+enjoyment+by+fostering+a+passion+in%22&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4) \- I think there is sometimes a shorter cut to yours. 'With ninety-nine men out of a hundred, a very sufficient dash of folly is necessary to render a woman piquante, a soft word for desirable; and, beyond these casual ebullitions of sympathy, few look for enjoyment by fostering a passion in their hearts. One reason, in short, why I wish my whole sex to become wiser, is, that the foolish ones may not, by their pretty folly, rob those whose sensibility keeps down their vanity, of the few roses that afford... ‎

[Appears in 15 books from 1879-2005](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&qtid=c27e78f5&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4)

[Page 4](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA4&vq=%22You+can+scarcely+imagine+with+what+pleasure+I+anticipate+the+day,+when+we+are+to+begin+almost+to+live+together%22&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4) \- You can scarcely imagine with what pleasure I anticipate the day, when we are to begin almost to live together ; and you would smile to hear how many plans of employment I have in my head, now that. I am confident my heart has found peace in your bosom. ‎

[Appears in 19 books from 1798-2001](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&qtid=cf7e78ea&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4)

[Page xxix](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&pg=PR29&vq=%22Let+woman+share+the+rights,+and+she+will+emulate+the+virtues+of+man+%3B+for+she+must+grow+more+perfect%22&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4) \- Let woman share the rights and she will emulate the virtues of man; for she must grow more perfect when emancipated, or justify the authority that chains such a weak being to her duty. ‎

[Appears in 65 books from 1857-2007](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&qtid=b37e78d7&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4)

[Page 29](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA29&vq=%22I+HAVE+just+received+your+kind+and+rational+letter,+and+would+fain+hide+my+face,+glowing+with+shame+for+my%22&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4) \- I HAVE just received your kind and rational letter, and would fain hide my face, glowing with shame for my folly. I would hide it in your bosom, if you would again open it to me, and nestle closely till you bade my fluttering heart be still, by saying that you forgave me. With eyes overflowing with tears, and in the humblest attitude, I entreat you. ‎

[Appears in 10 books from 1798-2003](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&qtid=467e78fe&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4)

[Page li](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&pg=PR51&vq=%22I+will+do+myself+the+pleasure+of+waiting+on+you+on+Friday,+and+shall+be+happy+to+meet+Mrs.+Wollstonecraft,%22&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4) \- I will do myself the pleasure of waiting on you Friday," he wrote, " and shall be happy to meet Mrs. Wollstonecraft, of whom I know not that I ever said a word of harm, and who has frequently amused herself with depreciating me. ‎

[Appears in 16 books from 1879-2003](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&qtid=107e78e0&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4)

[Page 87](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA87&vq=%22and+such+a+degree+of+respect+do+I+think+due+to+myself,+that,+if+only+probity,+which+is+a+good%22&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4) \- I consider fidelity and constancy as two distinct things; yet the former is necessary to give life to the other, and such a degree of respect do I think due to myself, that, if only probity, which is a good thing in its place, brings you back, never return... ‎

[Appears in 19 books from 1798-2003](https://books.google.com/books?id=S4RnAAAAMAAJ&qtid=107e7893&source=gbs_quotes_r&cad=4)
